fact: September 8 is exactly 251 days into the year, which means there are only 114 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime spider season - house spiders are frantically searching for mates and that's why you suddenly see them everywhere indoors!
The sun rises almost exactly due east and sets almost exactly due west around this date, as we're getting close to the autumn equinox when day and night are equal.
Monarch butterflies are typically in full migration mode right now, with some flying over 2,000 miles from Canada to Mexico - that's like flying from New York to Denver!
September 8 falls smack in the middle of Virgo season, making it one of the most 'Virgo-ish' days of the entire year.
In many northern places, morning dew becomes super heavy around this time because warm days meet suddenly cool nights, creating perfect conditions for water droplets to form on grass.
Squirrels go absolutely nuts (literally!) around September 8, frantically burying acorns and nuts for winter - a single squirrel can bury up to 10,000 nuts!
The moon often looks extra orange when it rises around this date in many places because of all the dust and particles kicked up during harvest season.
In the Southern Hemisphere, September 8 is like their version of March 8 - spring is springing and flowers are starting to bloom everywhere!
Apple trees typically hit their peak ripeness around this date in many northern regions, which is why 'apple picking season' is in full swing.
Geese start forming their famous V-shaped flying formations around now, honking loudly as they practice for their big journey south.
The temperature difference between day and night becomes really dramatic around September 8 in many places - you might wear shorts during the day and need a jacket at night!
